evals.prompt: Write a complete PRD for adding dark mode to our mobile banking app. Don't ask me any questions — use reasonable assumptions and produce the full document now. The target audience is mobile banking users who use the app at night. We want to ship this in about 6 weeks with a team of 2 engineers and 1 designer.
evals.expected_output: A complete PRD using the 12-section Shape Up + Working Backwards framework, including appetite with time budget, mock press release, Five Whys root cause analysis, rabbit holes, what we are NOT building, kill criteria, and a betting table with confidence level.

assertions.text: The PRD includes an explicit appetite or time budget using Shape Up language — either a small batch or big batch framing with a fixed time box — not just a generic timeline estimate or story points.
The PRD includes a mock press release headline or customer-facing announcement written as if the feature has already shipped, as part of a Working Backwards section.
The PRD has a dedicated rabbit holes section that names at least two specific technical or product complexities that could expand scope, with mitigation strategies.
The PRD explicitly calls out what is NOT being built in this cycle — scope exclusions presented as intentional decisions, not just omissions.
The PRD includes kill criteria — named conditions under which the team should abandon or pivot the bet.
The PRD includes a betting table or decision section with a confidence level expressed as a numeric score (e.g., 7/10) and a 'why now' rationale.

evals.prompt: Create a full product spec for letting customers split a restaurant bill inside our payments app. Don't ask clarifying questions — just produce the complete PRD with all sections filled in. Assume we're a mid-stage fintech startup, the feature was requested by 40% of surveyed users, and we want to keep scope tight.
evals.expected_output: A complete PRD with Shape Up appetite, Working Backwards press release and customer quote, Five Whys analysis, rabbit holes, scope exclusions, kill criteria, and betting table.

assertions.text: The PRD drills from a surface-level problem statement down to a root cause using an iterative why-based analysis — a chain of at least three levels of causation, not a single problem statement.
The PRD includes a fictional customer quote or testimonial written as a direct quote from a described user persona, as part of Working Backwards.
The PRD specifies an appetite using Shape Up terminology — small batch or big batch — with an explicit time box rather than a story point estimate or open-ended timeline.
The PRD identifies edge cases or complexity traps (rabbit holes) that the team should avoid or timebox, distinguishing them from the core solution shape.
The PRD lists explicit out-of-scope items framed as deliberate exclusions for this cycle.
The PRD includes a confidence or conviction score (numeric, e.g., 7/10) attached to the bet.

evals.prompt: Build a complete product requirements document for an AI writing assistant feature inside our SaaS project management tool. The feature should help project managers write status updates faster. Don't start a conversation — produce the entire PRD now with all sections. We're a 50-person company, this is a high-priority bet, and we have 6 weeks to ship.
evals.expected_output: A complete PRD using the 12-section framework, including Working Backwards, Five Whys, appetite, fat marker solution sketch, rabbit holes, scope exclusions, kill criteria, and betting table.

assertions.text: The PRD includes a Working Backwards artifact — a press release headline, customer quote, or internal quote — written from the perspective that the feature already exists.
The PRD frames scope in terms of a time budget (appetite) — a fixed-length cycle that constrains what gets built — rather than estimating how long the feature will take.
The PRD traces the problem from its observable symptom to a deeper root cause through iterative why reasoning, not just a single problem statement.
The solution section describes the feature at fat marker sketch level — broad strokes of the UX flow — without specifying pixel-level design or implementation details.
The PRD names at least two rabbit holes specific to AI features (e.g., prompt tuning scope, hallucination handling) and describes how to avoid or bound them.
The PRD specifies kill criteria — named conditions that would cause the team to drop the bet rather than continue.
